symptoms.symptom_name: Symmetrical, bilateral small-joint swelling and stiffness (both hands and both feet)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Symmetrical polyarticular small-joint involvement (MCP/PIP/MTP) is the classic distribution of an RA flare.
Septic arthritis is characteristically monoarticular; symmetrical bilateral polyarthritis argues strongly against a single infected joint.

symptoms.symptom_name: Early-morning stiffness lasting more than three hours

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Prolonged morning stiffness over an hour (here over three) is a hallmark of active inflammatory arthritis rather than mechanical or crystal disease.

symptoms.symptom_name: Subjective feverishness and systemic fatigue following a viral illness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Cytokine-driven constitutional symptoms and a viral trigger commonly accompany an RA flare.
Feverishness mandates active exclusion of joint or systemic infection - it is the reason the septic-arthritis mimic must be screened and safety-netted.
Constitutional upset after a viral illness keeps sepsis on the differential until rigors/high fever are excluded.
MTP-joint involvement causing forefoot pain on weight-bearing is a characteristic and often early RA feature.

symptoms.symptom_name: Known seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on hydroxychloroquine

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An established seropositive RA diagnosis makes a flare the prior-probable explanation for a symmetrical polyarthritis.
RA and its immunomodulating therapy also raise the baseline risk of joint infection, which is why the mimic cannot be dismissed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bedbound; unable to stand or walk safely; high falls risk

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Severe functional impairment reflects flare severity and drives the urgency of hands-on review and support, independent of the diagnosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: No single disproportionately hot, red, swollen joint (monoarticular pattern absent)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of a single hot, red, exquisitely tender joint is the key patient-reportable feature that lowers the probability of a septic joint (though it cannot exclude it without examination).

symptoms.symptom_name: No rigors and no measured high fever

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of rigors and of a measured high fever makes systemic sepsis less likely, though a measured temperature would strengthen the assessment.

symptoms.symptom_name: Symmetrical boggy synovitis with warmth and positive bilateral MCP/MTP squeeze test

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Boggy symmetrical synovitis and a positive bilateral squeeze test confirm active inflammatory polyarthritis - a clinician-only finding, sealed; telehealth cannot obtain it.
A symmetrical polyarticular pattern on examination (rather than one dominant hot joint) argues against a single infected joint.

symptoms.symptom_name: Low-grade measured temperature 37.6C with mild tachycardia (HR 98)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A low-grade temperature (not a spiking fever) is consistent with an inflammatory flare - a clinician-measured vital, sealed; the AI infers 'feverishness' only from patient report.
37.6C is not the high fever expected in significant systemic sepsis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR), full blood count and blood cultures

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Raised inflammatory markers support an active flare; markedly high markers with a left shift would raise concern for infection - a hospital/pathology investigation, never available in real time on telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Joint aspiration with synovial fluid microscopy, Gram stain and culture

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Synovial fluid analysis is the definitive test to confirm or exclude a septic joint - only performable in person, hence the need for urgent hands-on assessment.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Together, symmetrical bilateral small-joint involvement, prolonged morning stiffness, forefoot MTP pain and a known seropositive RA diagnosis strongly favour a flare over an infective mimic.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: septic arthritis / systemic sepsis (screened out for now)
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: No single hot joint and no rigors/high fever lower the immediate probability of joint or systemic infection on patient-reportable features - permitting prompt in-person review rather than emergency escalation, while these remain the safety-net triggers.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person joint examination including bilateral squeeze test and assessment for a monoarticular hot joint
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The pivotal step: distinguishes symmetrical inflammatory synovitis from a single septic joint; cannot be done over telehealth.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: CRP, ESR, full blood count and blood cultures
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Gauges the degree of inflammation and screens for infection before any immunosuppressive steroid is started.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Joint aspiration with synovial fluid microscopy, Gram stain and culture
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Definitive test if any joint is suspicious for infection; performed in person if the examination raises concern.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Repeat/serial serology or advanced imaging in the acute telehealth encounter
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not appropriate to order remotely in this acute encounter; assessment and bedside tests belong to the in-person review, not a telehealth work-up.
